Interpretation: Despite ToT deterioration (exports becoming relatively cheaper), the large volume expansion (150%) more than compensated, resulting in 112.5% rise in real purchasing power. Volume effects dominated terms of trade effects — a key defence of export-led growth strategies.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why agricultural reform was the 'missing link' in India's 1991 LPG reforms?
options: Agricultural reforms were not needed as India was already a food surplus nation
LPG reforms focused on industry and services but neglected agriculture — APMC Acts, MSP distortions, export bans, and lack of supply chain investment kept agriculture uncompetitive globally
Agricultural sector was reserved for the public sector and could not be liberalised
International trade rules (WTO) prohibited India from reforming its agricultural sector
questions.explanation: Agricultural reform as the missing link: India's LPG reforms comprehensively liberalised industry and services but agriculture remained heavily regulated: (1) APMC (Agricultural Produce Market Committee) Acts restricted market access and private investment in mandis, (2) MSP (Minimum Support Price) created price distortions, (3) Export bans on agricultural commodities reduced farmer incomes and global market integration, (4) Restriction on private warehousing and futures trading, (5) FDI restrictions in retail prevented modern supply chains. Result: India's farm productivity remained below potential, rural incomes grew slowly, and food inflation remained chronic. The 2020 Farm Laws attempted to address some of these gaps (though subsequently repealed).

questions.question: The 'Balance of Payments' (BoP) crisis that triggered India's 1991 reforms occurred when:
options: India's stock market crashed and FII investments fled
India could not meet its international payment obligations due to depleted foreign exchange reserves
India's fiscal deficit exceeded 10% of GDP
India defaulted on its domestic government bond obligations
questions.explanation: A Balance of Payments crisis occurs when a country cannot meet its international financial obligations — paying for imports, repaying external debt, and meeting other foreign exchange commitments. In 1991, India's forex reserves fell to approximately $1 billion — equivalent to only 2 weeks of imports — making it impossible to continue normal import payments. India was on the verge of defaulting on international loans. This precipitated the emergency reforms. India pledged 67 tonnes of gold (to Union Bank of Switzerland and Bank of England) as collateral for emergency loans before approaching IMF.

questions.question: If India's import bill was $27 billion in 1990-91 and foreign exchange reserves were $1.1 billion, how many weeks of import coverage did India have? (Assume 52 weeks in a year)
options: 2.1 weeks
4 weeks
8 weeks
1.5 weeks
questions.explanation: Weekly import requirement = Annual imports / 52 = $27 billion / 52 = $0.519 billion per week. Weeks of import coverage = Forex reserves / Weekly import requirement = $1.1 billion / $0.519 billion = 2.12 weeks ≈ 2.1 weeks. This critically low reserve level is what triggered the 1991 crisis. The standard safe level of forex reserves is 3 months (12 weeks) of imports. Having only 2 weeks meant India was in acute BoP distress. This calculation provides the quantitative context for the 1991 crisis.

questions.question: The 'Dunkel Draft' refers to which of the following in the context of India's globalisation?
options: A draft agreement on bilateral trade between India and the European Union
The draft text of the Final Act of the Uruguay Round (GATT) negotiations, presented by GATT Director General Arthur Dunkel in 1991
A World Bank conditionality document for India's structural adjustment loans
The Narasimham Committee draft report on banking sector reforms
questions.explanation: The Dunkel Draft was the comprehensive draft text of the Uruguay Round trade agreement, presented by GATT Director General Arthur Dunkel in December 1991. It covered: goods trade (tariff reductions), agricultural trade reforms, TRIPS (intellectual property), TRIMS (investment measures), services (GATS), dispute settlement, and establishment of WTO. It was highly controversial in India — critics argued it would hurt Indian farmers (agricultural liberalisation), generic drug manufacturers (TRIPS/patents), and allow MNCs unrestricted access (TRIMS). Its eventual adoption as part of Marrakesh Agreement (1994) established WTO and shaped India's globalisation trajectory.

questions.question: India's pharmaceutical sector experience with TRIPS (Trade-Related Intellectual Property Rights) under WTO illustrates which fundamental tension in globalisation?
options: Tension between India's desire for high-value exports and WTO's requirements for free trade
Tension between global IP protection (benefiting innovator companies in developed countries) and public health access (requiring affordable generic medicines in developing countries)
Tension between domestic patent law and international copyright regulations
Tension between India's agricultural interests and pharmaceutical manufacturing interests
questions.explanation: TRIPS and pharmaceutical access: Pre-TRIPS, India's Patents Act 1970 did not allow product patents on pharmaceuticals (only process patents), enabling Indian generic drug manufacturers to make affordable versions of patented drugs. After TRIPS (implemented by India in 2005): Full product patents required, restricting generic production of patented medicines. Tension: (1) TRIPS benefits innovator pharma companies (mostly from developed countries) by protecting their R&D investments, (2) BUT product patents increase drug prices dramatically, restricting access in poor countries (AIDS drugs, cancer treatments). India's response — Section 3(d) of Patents Act (anti-evergreening provision) and compulsory licensing provisions — attempted to balance innovation protection with public health. The 2013 Novartis/Glivec case exemplified this tension globally.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's IT sector success is partly a result of globalisation but was not directly caused by the 1991 LPG reform package. Reason (R): India's IT boom was driven by a combination of pre-existing factors (IIT-trained engineers, English proficiency) and the US tech boom (Y2K, dot-com era demand) rather than exclusively by tariff reduction or FDI liberalisation.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false — IT growth was entirely due to 1991 reforms
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is TRUE: The IT sector's takeoff was not directly the result of tariff reductions or industrial deregulation — India's IT sector was export-oriented and technology-focused, not dependent on import protection removal. Reason R correctly explains this: The critical factors were: (1) IIT and NIT graduates from the 1970s-80s providing skilled manpower, (2) US demand shock from Y2K remediation (1995-1999) and dot-com boom, (3) Reduction in telecommunications costs (satellite bandwidth), (4) English language advantage. Deregulation helped (removing software export restrictions) but was not the primary driver. The IT success was partly 'good luck' (Y2K) combined with pre-existing human capital. R correctly identifies these non-reform factors.

questions.question: The concept of 'immiserising growth' (Bhagwati, 1958) would apply to India's trade liberalisation if:
options: India's growth makes all Indians poorer in absolute terms
India's export growth deteriorates its terms of trade so severely that despite higher export volumes, the country's real income (purchasing power) falls
India's poor become absolutely poorer even while GDP grows
India's growth is concentrated exclusively in urban areas, impoverishing rural India
questions.explanation: Immiserising Growth (Jagdish Bhagwati, 1958): A theoretical possibility where a large country's export expansion causes its terms of trade to deteriorate so much that despite growing export volumes, real national income falls. Conditions: (1) Country is large enough to affect world prices with its exports, (2) Demand for its exports is inelastic, (3) Terms of trade deterioration > gains from volume expansion. Applied to India: If India greatly expanded agricultural commodity exports, it might depress world commodity prices (India as a large player in rice, wheat) — TOT worsening could potentially reduce real income. However, India's actual experience showed volume gains dominated TOT effects (as shown in Q74). The concept is more relevant to primary commodity exporters in Africa.

questions.question: India's disinvestment proceeds target was ₹40,000 crore in 2015-16. If the government disinvested 10% equity in a PSU valued at ₹2,00,000 crore, what was the expected revenue from this single disinvestment? What percentage of the annual target does this represent?
options: Revenue = ₹20,000 crore; 50% of target
Revenue = ₹2,000 crore; 5% of target
Revenue = ₹10,000 crore; 25% of target
Revenue = ₹4,000 crore; 10% of target
questions.explanation: Revenue from disinvestment = 10% of PSU value = 10% × ₹2,00,000 crore = ₹20,000 crore. As percentage of annual target = (₹20,000 / ₹40,000) × 100 = 50% of target. This single disinvestment alone meets half the government's annual target. In practice, India's disinvestment proceeds have fallen short of budgeted targets in many years due to market conditions (low equity valuations), procedural delays, and political opposition. The dependence on one or two large disinvestments makes the programme vulnerable to market timing.

questions.question: In a Production Possibility Curve (PPC) diagram, how would India's LPG reforms theoretically shift the curve over time?
options: The PPC contracts inward as resources shift to the service sector
The PPC expands outward as technology transfer, capital accumulation through FDI, and efficiency gains from competition expand productive capacity
The PPC rotates, favouring services at the expense of manufacturing
The PPC remains unchanged as LPG reforms only affect distribution, not production
questions.explanation: LPG reforms' impact on the PPC: The PPC represents the maximum possible output combinations given existing technology and resources. LPG reforms expand the PPC through: (1) Technology transfer from FDI — improved production methods increase productivity of existing resources, (2) Capital deepening — FDI and domestic investment increase capital per worker, (3) Efficiency gains — competition (from trade and private entry) eliminates X-inefficiency, moving production from inside the PPC to the frontier, (4) Skill development through education-reform linkages. While there may be structural shifts (favoring services), the overall effect is expansion — India's GDP doubling approximately every 10 years post-reform reflects PPC expansion.

questions.question: In 1997, Southeast Asian economies (Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea) faced severe currency crises despite strong fundamentals. India was largely unaffected. What feature of India's post-1991 reforms explains this resilience?
options: India's high import tariffs protecting the domestic economy from external shocks
India's maintained capital controls on portfolio investment and partial current account convertibility limiting speculative attack vulnerability
India's large gold reserves preventing currency devaluation
India's membership in ASEAN providing regional economic support
questions.explanation: India's resilience in the 1997 Asian Crisis: Southeast Asian countries had liberalised their capital accounts (fully open), adopted quasi-fixed exchange rates, and accumulated large short-term foreign debt. Speculative attacks triggered massive capital flight, currency crashes, and economic collapse. India avoided this because: (1) Capital account NOT fully convertible — RBI maintained controls on portfolio investment flows (FII entry/exit regulated, external commercial borrowings restricted), (2) Exchange rate was managed float, not fixed — no hard peg to defend, (3) External debt composition: India's external debt was predominantly long-term, not short-term hot money. The IMF subsequently acknowledged that India's cautious approach was prudent. This is the strongest empirical argument for India's gradual approach to globalisation.

questions.question: The 'Uruguay Round' of GATT negotiations (1986-1994) was significant for India because it:
options: Was a bilateral trade agreement between India and Uruguay
Led to establishment of WTO, extended trade rules to agriculture and services, introduced TRIPS — fundamentally reshaping India's trade and industrial policy obligations
Gave India preferential market access in European markets
Allowed India to maintain import quotas on agricultural products indefinitely
questions.explanation: The Uruguay Round (launched 1986, concluded 1994 in Marrakesh) was the most comprehensive trade negotiation in history, fundamentally affecting India: (1) WTO established — binding dispute settlement, more rigorous compliance requirements, (2) Agreement on Agriculture — reduction commitments on subsidies and border protection (though with special and differential treatment for developing countries), (3) TRIPS — India had to amend patents law to allow pharmaceutical product patents, (4) GATS — services trade rules affecting India's IT exports and Mode 4 (movement of professionals), (5) TRIMS — investment measures related to trade. India signed as a founding member, accepting these obligations as part of globalisation.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's FDI inflows remained below potential despite liberalisation due to infrastructural bottlenecks, rigid labour laws, and bureaucratic hurdles. Reason (R): Ease of Doing Business rankings (World Bank) consistently placed India in lower positions (ranked 100+ for many years) compared to China and ASEAN competitors, reflecting these structural impediments.
options: Both A and R are true, and R provides quantitative evidence supporting A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT relevant to A
A is false — India received maximum FDI among developing countries
A is true but R is false — India consistently ranked in top 50 for ease of business
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and connected. Despite FDI liberalisation (automatic approval for up to 74-100% in most sectors), India consistently received less FDI than comparable economies: China received 5-10x more FDI annually in the 2000s. Reasons: (1) Infrastructure deficit — unreliable power, poor roads, congested ports, (2) Labour market rigidity — Industrial Disputes Act requiring government approval for layoffs in firms with 100+ workers, (3) Land acquisition complexity — multiple land owners, zoning restrictions, (4) Contract enforcement — judicial delays, (5) Multiple inspectors and compliances. World Bank's Ease of Doing Business Index captured these — India ranked 142 in 2014, improving to 63 by 2019 after Modi government's reform push. R is empirical evidence for A.

questions.question: India's trade-to-GDP ratio was 15% in 1990-91 and rose to 45% by 2010-11. If India's GDP grew from $300 billion to $1,700 billion in this period, calculate the absolute increase in trade (exports + imports). What does this indicate about globalisation's depth?
options: Trade increased from $45 billion to $765 billion — an increase of $720 billion, showing trade grew faster than GDP
Trade increased from $45 billion to $500 billion — an increase of $455 billion
Trade increased from $300 billion to $765 billion
Trade remained unchanged as trade-to-GDP ratio captures relative, not absolute, change
questions.explanation: Trade (Exports + Imports) = Trade-to-GDP ratio × GDP. In 1990-91: Trade = 15% × $300 billion = $45 billion. In 2010-11: Trade = 45% × $1,700 billion = $765 billion. Absolute increase = $765 − $45 = $720 billion. Interpretation: Trade grew by 17x ($45 billion to $765 billion) while GDP grew by 5.67x ($300 billion to $1,700 billion). Trade growing faster than GDP confirms DEEP globalisation — India's integration with the world economy accelerated the absolute trade expansion even beyond GDP growth. The rise in trade-to-GDP ratio from 15% to 45% quantifies this deepening integration.

questions.question: The 'EXIM Policy' (Export-Import Policy) reform post-1991 significantly impacted India's trade by:
options: Imposing strict import quotas to protect domestic industries
Simplifying import procedures, reducing negative list items, abolishing import licensing for most goods, and creating export promotion schemes
Nationalising India's export-import financing institutions
Restricting imports from countries with which India had bilateral trade deficits
questions.explanation: Post-1991 EXIM Policy reforms: (1) Negative list (prohibited/restricted imports) drastically reduced — from hundreds of items to a limited list, (2) Import licensing abolished for most consumer goods (except sensitive items), (3) Export promotion schemes — Duty Drawback, DEPB (Duty Entitlement Pass Book), EOU (Export Oriented Units), EPZ/SEZ, (4) Advance Licensing — importing duty-free inputs for export production, (5) EPCG scheme — duty-free capital goods import for export commitment. These reforms made India's trade regime more market-oriented, reducing transaction costs and promoting export competitiveness.

questions.question: The concept of 'Policy Space' in the context of LPG reforms and developing countries refers to:
options: Physical office space allocated to economic policy departments
The ability of developing country governments to use industrial policy, trade policy, and subsidies for development — which WTO rules and IMF conditionalities may constrain
Space available for new economic policies in the government's annual budget
The range of monetary policy tools available to developing country central banks
questions.explanation: Policy Space refers to the room developing country governments have to design and implement heterodox economic policies (e.g., infant industry protection, strategic subsidies, capital controls, public investment). LPG and WTO membership constrains this: (1) WTO rules limit subsidy regimes, import protection instruments, and local content requirements (TRIMS), (2) IMF loan conditionalities restrict fiscal deficits and monetary policy, (3) BIT (Bilateral Investment Treaties) limit regulatory sovereignty. Critics like Ha-Joon Chang argue that developed countries 'kicked away the ladder' by using these policies during their own development but now deny them through WTO/IMF to developing countries. This is the core critique of the current international economic order from a development perspective.

questions.question: If India's export subsidy was ₹5,000 crore annually and WTO rules required elimination, calculate the net welfare impact on India's economy. Assume: exporters' revenue falls by ₹4,000 crore, government saves ₹5,000 crore, consumers gain ₹0 (no domestic market impact), and trading partners gain ₹1,000 crore from lower subsidised competition.
options: Net welfare gain for India = ₹1,000 crore
Net welfare loss for India = ₹4,000 crore
Net welfare gain for India = ₹5,000 crore
Net welfare impact is zero — transfers within the economy
questions.explanation: Export subsidy elimination welfare analysis for India: Government savings = +₹5,000 crore. Exporter revenue loss = −₹4,000 crore. Net domestic welfare impact = +₹5,000 − ₹4,000 = +₹1,000 crore gain for India. The ₹5,000 crore subsidy was a transfer from taxpayers (government) to exporters. Net welfare: Only ₹4,000 crore was genuinely needed by exporters (their actual revenue loss); the remaining ₹1,000 crore was a deadweight loss (pure waste/administrative cost) that is now saved. Trading partners also gain ₹1,000 crore globally. This illustrates the standard result that export subsidies are economically inefficient even for the subsidising country — WTO rules against them have economic justification.

questions.question: The 'TRIMS Agreement' (Trade-Related Investment Measures) under WTO prohibits which of the following Indian policy instruments?
options: Requiring foreign companies to pay corporate taxes in India
Mandatory local content requirements (requiring foreign investors to source a percentage of inputs domestically)
Requiring foreign companies to hire a minimum percentage of local employees
Setting environmental standards for foreign manufacturing units
questions.explanation: TRIMS Agreement prohibits investment measures that distort trade: (1) Local Content Requirements — mandating that a specified percentage of inputs be sourced domestically (this was a key tool of India's industrial policy, requiring automobile manufacturers to use locally made components). (2) Trade balancing requirements — tying import rights to export earnings. (3) Foreign exchange balancing — requiring investors to balance import spending with export earnings. India had extensive local content requirements in automobile, defence, and electronics sectors. WTO/TRIMS compliance required phasing these out. India's 'Make in India' policy had to carefully design domestic procurement preferences to be TRIMS-compatible.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's financial sector reform included allowing foreign banks to operate in India and Indian banks to expand globally. Reason (R): Reciprocity in financial services trade under GATS required India to open its banking sector in exchange for market access for Indian financial firms abroad.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the primary explanation — domestic efficiency rationale was equally important
A is true but R is false — India opened banking voluntarily without reciprocity
A is false — foreign banks were prohibited from operating in India post-1991
questions.explanation: Assertion A is TRUE: India's banking reform did include allowing more foreign banks (subject to RBI conditions: minimum capital, priority sector lending, financial inclusion norms) and facilitating Indian banks' international expansion. Reason R is PARTIALLY true (GATS does include financial services schedule and reciprocity), but the PRIMARY motivation for banking liberalisation was DOMESTIC EFFICIENCY: competition from foreign banks was expected to improve service quality, technology adoption, and customer focus in Indian banks. Additionally, RBI's capital adequacy and prudential norms (Basel Accord compliance) required modernisation. Reciprocity under GATS was a contributing factor but not the primary explanation — hence B is correct.

questions.question: During COVID-19 pandemic (2020-21), India experienced sharp reversal of FII flows: FIIs withdrew ₹1,12,000 crore from Indian markets in March 2020. Subsequently, unprecedented monetary expansion by US Fed drove $30 billion of FII inflows into India in October-December 2020. This scenario best illustrates which concept from India's globalisation experience?
options: That FDI is more stable than FII and India should prioritise FDI

The inherent volatility of portfolio capital flows (hot money) — driven by global factors (US monetary policy, risk appetite) rather than Indian fundamentals — creating macroeconomic management challenges
That India should immediately implement full capital account convertibility
That COVID demonstrated globalisation's failure and India should return to autarky
questions.explanation: The COVID FII experience perfectly illustrates hot money dynamics: March 2020 withdrawal (₹1,12,000 crore): Triggered by global risk-off sentiment, dollar liquidity crunch, not India-specific deterioration. Rupee depreciated, stock market crashed 35%. October-December 2020 inflows ($30 billion): Triggered by US Fed's $3 trillion asset purchase programme creating excess dollar liquidity seeking emerging market yields. Not related to India's improving fundamentals. This demonstrates: (1) FII flows are driven by global monetary conditions, not just India factors, (2) Large swings create exchange rate volatility, inflation risk, and asset price bubbles, (3) Capital controls help buffer these shocks. It validates India's cautious approach to capital account convertibility — exactly the challenge the Tarapore Committee anticipated.

questions.question: A developing country moves from autarky to free trade. Pre-trade: domestic price = ₹200, quantity = 100 units. Post-trade: world price = ₹150, domestic quantity demanded = 130 units, domestic production = 80 units, imports = 50 units. Calculate: (i) Consumer surplus gain, (ii) Producer surplus loss, (iii) Net welfare gain.
options: CS gain = ₹5,750; PS loss = ₹4,500; Net gain = ₹1,250
CS gain = ₹3,000; PS loss = ₹1,000; Net gain = ₹2,000
CS gain = ₹2,500; PS loss = ₹2,500; Net gain = ₹0
CS gain = ₹4,000; PS loss = ₹500; Net gain = ₹3,500
questions.explanation: Price falls by ₹50. Consumer surplus gain is the trapezium under demand: ₹50 x (100 + 130) / 2 = ₹5,750. Producer surplus loss is the trapezium over supply: ₹50 x (100 + 80) / 2 = ₹4,500. Net welfare gain = ₹5,750 - ₹4,500 = ₹1,250. Option A is correct.

questions.question: The 'Stolper-Samuelson Theorem' predicts that in a labour-abundant country like India, trade liberalisation should INCREASE wages. Why did India's trade liberalisation not uniformly increase wages for unskilled workers?
options: The theorem was theoretically correct and wages did rise for all Indian workers
India did not liberalise trade sufficiently to trigger the Stolper-Samuelson effect

India's trade liberalisation was sector-specific (IT/skilled labour), rigid labour markets prevented wage adjustment, and China's competition limited labour-intensive manufacturing growth — all violating Stolper-Samuelson assumptions
The theorem applies only to capital-abundant countries
questions.explanation: Stolper-Samuelson predicts: Trade liberalisation in a labour-abundant country increases wages (return to abundant factor). Why India violated this: (1) Sector mismatch — India's gains concentrated in skill-intensive IT (benefiting skilled workers/human capital, not unskilled labour), (2) China factor — China's entry into global labour markets (post-WTO 2001) at even lower wages prevented India's unskilled labour from capturing manufacturing export opportunities, (3) Labour market rigidities — Industrial Disputes Act made formal employment costly, pushing workers to informal sector with lower wages, (4) Capital-skill complementarity — FDI brought capital-intensive technology, increasing skill premium. These factors explain why Stolper-Samuelson's prediction didn't uniformly materialise for unskilled workers.

questions.question: What was the primary objective of reducing the 'fiscal deficit' as part of India's 1991 stabilisation programme?
options: To increase public sector investment in infrastructure
To reduce government borrowing, lower interest rates, reduce inflation, and restore macroeconomic stability
To increase tax rates to generate more revenue
To reduce India's trade deficit with major trading partners
questions.explanation: Reducing fiscal deficit was central to IMF's stabilisation prescription: High fiscal deficit (8.4% of GDP in 1990-91) caused: (1) High government borrowing → high interest rates → crowding out private investment, (2) Monetisation of deficit (RBI printing money) → inflation (13% in 1991), (3) External debt accumulation → BoP crisis, (4) Reduced credit for private sector. By reducing fiscal deficit: government borrowing fell → interest rates declined → private investment stimulated → inflation controlled → BoP improved. This macroeconomic stabilisation was the short-term component of reform, complementing structural reforms (liberalisation, privatisation) for medium-term growth.

questions.question: If India's Wholesale Price Index (WPI) was 180 in 1991 (base 1981=100) and IMF target was to reduce inflation to single digits within 2 years, by what level should WPI be limited to if it started at 180 and target inflation is 8%?
options: WPI ≤ 194.4 after 1 year, WPI ≤ 209.9 after 2 years
WPI ≤ 194.4 after 1 year, WPI ≤ 180 after 2 years
WPI ≤ 170 after 2 years
WPI ≤ 200 after 2 years
questions.explanation: Inflation target calculation: Starting WPI = 180. Year 1 target (8% inflation): WPI Year 1 = 180 × (1 + 0.08) = 180 × 1.08 = 194.4. Year 2 target (8% inflation on Year 1): WPI Year 2 = 194.4 × (1 + 0.08) = 194.4 × 1.08 = 209.95 ≈ 209.9. If actual WPI exceeds 194.4 after Year 1 or 209.9 after Year 2, the 8% inflation target is breached. This illustrates how inflation targeting works in practice — each year's price level must not rise faster than the target rate. India did successfully reduce WPI inflation from 13% (1991) to 8% (1993) and further, meeting IMF stabilisation targets.

questions.question: The 'Heckscher-Ohlin' theory of international trade predicts India should export goods that are intensive in India's abundant factor. In 1991, India's most abundant factor relative to other trading partners was:
options: Capital — India had high savings rates and well-developed capital markets
Land — India had large agricultural land relative to its trading partners
Labour — India had a large, relatively low-cost labour force
Technology — India had strong R&D capabilities
questions.explanation: In 1991, India's most abundant factor of production (relative to trading partners, especially OECD countries) was LABOUR — a large working-age population with relatively low wage rates compared to developed economies. H-O theory predicts India should have comparative advantage in labour-intensive exports (textiles, garments, leather goods, basic electronics assembly). This is indeed the basis of India's traditional export comparative advantage. However, India's actual export success post-1991 was more in skill-intensive services (IT) and pharmaceuticals — partly validating and partly complicating the simple H-O prediction for India.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India should have pursued a more gradual approach to trade liberalisation like China (which retained state control over trade and currency) rather than the rapid IMF-prescribed approach. Reason (R): China's export-led industrialisation strategy, maintained under state direction with targeted protection and currency management, resulted in stronger manufacturing sector development compared to India's approach.
options: Both A and R are true, and R supports A as empirical evidence
Both A and R are true, but R does not support A — different contexts made strategies non-transferable
A is false — India's approach was superior as demonstrated by IT sector success
R is true but A is false — China's model cannot be replicated in a democracy
questions.explanation: This is a genuinely contested development economics question. Assertion A has merit: A more gradual, state-directed approach (as in East Asian 'developmental state' models) might have better supported manufacturing. Reason R is empirically true: China's manufacturing GDP share reached 30%+ while India stagnated at 15-16%. However, B is also defensible: (1) India and China had vastly different starting conditions in 1991 — China had already completed rural collectivisation reform (1978-1990), (2) India's democratic constraints made industrial policy command-and-control impossible, (3) India's crisis was acute (2 weeks forex reserves) — gradualism was not feasible when immediate IMF assistance was needed. Context-dependency makes simple comparison misleading.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST comprehensive and accurate assessment of India's LPG reforms since 1991?
options: Unambiguous success — GDP growth, poverty reduction, and globalisation integration demonstrate complete success
Complete failure — inequality, jobless growth, and agricultural distress prove reforms hurt India

Mixed outcome — significant achievements in growth, integration, and poverty reduction, but uneven sectoral performance (services > manufacturing), rising inequality, agricultural neglect, and infrastructural gaps remain
Premature assessment — insufficient time has passed to evaluate 1991 reforms
questions.explanation: A balanced evaluation of India's LPG reforms: ACHIEVEMENTS: (1) GDP growth acceleration from 3.5% to 6-7%+ average, (2) Poverty reduction (45% to 21.9% headcount), (3) Forex reserves from $1 billion to $600 billion, (4) IT sector — global leadership, (5) Export diversification and growth. SHORTCOMINGS: (1) Manufacturing sector weakness — only 15-16% of GDP, (2) Rising inequality — Gini rose, (3) Jobless growth in formal sector, (4) Agricultural distress — farmer incomes, (5) Infrastructure deficit persisting, (6) Regional disparities widened (Southern/Western states benefited more). NUANCES: Democratic constraints, China comparison, incomplete reforms (land, labour, agriculture). A comprehensive assessment acknowledges both dimensions — hence Option C is correct.

questions.question: The 'New Economic Policy' (NEP) 1991 in India is distinguished from earlier economic policies primarily by:
options: Introducing social welfare programmes for the poor
Shifting from a state-led, protected economy to a market-oriented, globally integrated economy
Nationalising private sector banks and industries
Increasing defence expenditure to strengthen national security
questions.explanation: India's New Economic Policy (NEP) 1991 represented a fundamental paradigm shift: PRE-1991 (Nehruvian socialist model): State-led development, heavy industry focus, extensive public sector, import substitution, high protection, licence raj, controlled foreign exchange. POST-1991 (NEP): Market-oriented resource allocation, private sector-led growth, trade openness, FDI welcome, deregulation, financial liberalisation. The shift was from 'command economy' elements toward market economy — not full laissez-faire (government retained a role) but a major departure from the socialist planning model that had dominated since 1947. This paradigm shift is the defining characteristic of NEP 1991.

questions.question: India's public sector's share in GDP was 26% in 1991 with 2.3 crore employees. Post-reforms, public sector share fell to 20% of GDP while GDP doubled. If private sector employment grew to fill the gap, and private sector GDP-employment ratio is 2x more efficient than public sector, how many private sector jobs were created to produce the output previously produced by the public sector?
options: 0.575 crore private jobs replaced 1.15 crore public jobs
1.15 crore private jobs replaced 2.3 crore public jobs
2.3 crore private jobs replaced 2.3 crore public jobs
0.575 crore private jobs only partially replaced public sector output
questions.explanation: Public sector GDP contribution in 1991 = 26% of GDP. After GDP doubled: if public sector retained 20% of 2×GDP, public sector output increased despite shrinking share. The portion of output 'shifted' to private sector = (26% − 20% adjusted for GDP growth). For simplification: public sector employment efficiency ratio vs private = 1:2. If public sector employed 2.3 crore for 26% of GDP, private sector needs only 1.15 crore to produce equivalent output (due to 2x efficiency). This illustrates a key privatisation argument: private sector productivity allows same output with fewer workers, but the displaced 1.15 crore workers need to find alternative employment — the social cost of efficiency gain.

questions.question: India's introduction of the 'Goods and Services Tax' (GST) in 2017 can be seen as a continuation of which 1991 reform philosophy?
options: Privatisation — GST reduced government's role in tax collection
Liberalisation — GST unified India's fragmented internal tax system, creating a single market and reducing the 'tax as non-tariff barrier' effect on interstate trade
Globalisation — GST was required under WTO agreements
Fiscal expansion — GST increased government revenue to fund social programmes
questions.explanation: GST as a continuation of liberalisation: Pre-GST, India had a complex, cascading tax system with Central excise, State VAT, CST (on interstate trade), service tax, entry taxes, octroi — creating multiple compliance burdens and making interstate movement of goods costly. This was essentially an internal trade barrier. GST unified these into one system: (1) Eliminated cascading taxes (reducing production costs), (2) Removed CST (facilitating free interstate trade — domestic market integration), (3) Standardised tax structure (reducing compliance burden). This is directly analogous to external trade liberalisation (removing tariffs) but applied to internal trade — completing India's internal single market, which supports the liberalisation philosophy of removing unnecessary economic barriers.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's MSME (Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises) sector faced significant challenges due to globalisation-induced import competition post-1991. Reason (R): MSMEs typically have lower capital, technology, and scale compared to global competitors, making them vulnerable to cost-competitive imported products once tariff protection is reduced.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false — MSMEs benefited from globalisation through export opportunities
A is true but R is false — MSMEs failed only due to domestic policy neglect
questions.explanation: Both Assertion A and Reason R are correct. India's MSME sector (employing over 11 crore workers) faced severe competitive pressure post-liberalisation, particularly from Chinese imports in sectors like toys, electronics, footwear, and plastic goods. R correctly explains this: MSMEs' structural disadvantages — limited capital, older technology, small scale (no economies of scale), and poor logistics access — made them unable to match prices of large-scale, modern foreign manufacturers. China's state support and massive scale further intensified the problem. Policy response: Technology Upgradation Funds, MSME clusters, Udyam portal registration, and procurement preferences tried to mitigate this structural disadvantage.

questions.question: In a graph plotting FDI inflows (Y-axis) against Ease of Doing Business Rank (X-axis, where lower rank = better), what relationship would economic theory predict, and how does India's empirical experience from 2010-2019 support this?
options: Positive relationship (higher FDI with worse rank) — contradicting theory
No relationship — FDI is determined only by market size

Negative relationship — as Ease of Doing Business rank improves (falls), FDI inflows increase, consistent with India's experience where rank improved from 142 to 63 while FDI grew from $24 billion to $51 billion
U-shaped relationship — both very good and very poor business environments attract FDI
questions.explanation: Economic theory predicts a negative relationship between Ease of Doing Business rank number and FDI (as rank number falls/improves, FDI rises): Lower rank number = better business environment → Lower transaction costs, reduced uncertainty → Higher expected returns on investment → More FDI. India's empirical experience (2014-2019): World Bank EoDB rank improved from 142 (2014) to 63 (2019) — a 79-position improvement. FDI inflows grew from approximately $24 billion (2013-14) to $51 billion (2019-20). This positive correlation supports theoretical prediction. Causality: reforms driving rank improvement (labour code, insolvency law, GST, single window) simultaneously reduced investment costs and attracted FDI.

questions.question: Village Bhanpur is an agricultural community. Due to globalisation, genetically modified (GM) seeds from a US multinational become available at lower cost but require annual purchase (no re-sowing). A farmer switches, yields increase 40%, but costs also rise by ₹15,000/acre. If farmer's revenue increases by ₹20,000/acre and previous seed cost was ₹5,000/acre, calculate net annual change in farmer's profit per acre.
options: Profit increases by ₹10,000/acre
Profit increases by ₹5,000/acre
Profit increases by ₹20,000/acre
Profit decreases by ₹5,000/acre
questions.explanation: Revenue increases by ₹20,000 per acre and costs rise by ₹15,000 per acre. Net change in profit = ₹20,000 - ₹15,000 = ₹5,000 per acre. The earlier seed cost is background information; the relevant comparison is the increase in revenue against the increase in cost. Option B is correct.

questions.question: The 'Chinn-Ito Index' (KAOPEN) measures a country's degree of capital account openness. India has consistently scored LOWER than advanced economies on this index. What does this indicate about India's globalisation strategy?
options: India has a more open capital account than its peers
India has deliberately maintained capital controls, pursuing managed globalisation rather than full financial openness
India's lower score reflects poor economic management
The index measures trade openness, not capital flows
questions.explanation: The Chinn-Ito Index measures de jure capital account openness (0 = completely closed to 1 = completely open). India's KAOPEN score typically around 0.15-0.20 indicates maintained capital controls, compared to USA/UK at 1.0 or Singapore/Hong Kong near 1.0. This reflects India's deliberate strategy: (1) Gradual, sequenced financial liberalisation, (2) Caution following Asian Financial Crisis (1997), (3) Prioritising monetary policy independence (Mundell-Fleming trilemma), (4) RBI concerns about financial stability. This 'managed globalisation' approach allowed India to attract FDI (long-term) while limiting volatile portfolio flows — providing resilience during global financial crises of 1997, 2008, and 2020.

questions.question: India's Gross Capital Formation (GCF) as % of GDP rose from 22% in 1991 to 38% in 2007. If GDP was $300 billion in 1991 and $1,200 billion in 2007, calculate the absolute increase in investment. If FDI constituted 5% of GCF in 2007, what was FDI in absolute terms?
options: GCF increase = $390 billion; FDI = $22.8 billion
GCF increase = $390 billion; FDI = $5 billion
GCF increase = $522 billion; FDI = $22.8 billion
GCF increase = $456 billion; FDI = $22 billion
questions.explanation: GCF in 1991 = 22% × $300 billion = $66 billion. GCF in 2007 = 38% × $1,200 billion = $456 billion. Absolute increase in investment = $456 − $66 = $390 billion. FDI as % of GCF in 2007 = 5%. FDI = 5% × $456 billion = $22.8 billion. This calculation reveals: (1) India's investment rate nearly doubled (22% to 38% of GDP) — a key driver of economic acceleration, (2) FDI, despite growing from near-zero to $22.8 billion, still constituted only 5% of total investment — domestic investment was the primary source, (3) Gross domestic savings financed the majority of India's investment surge — refuting the idea that FDI was the engine of India's growth.

questions.question: Which finance minister presented India's liberalisation budget in July 1991, stating 'there is no time to lose' in his budget speech?
options: P. Chidambaram
Yashwant Sinha
Manmohan Singh
Pranab Mukherjee
questions.explanation: Dr. Manmohan Singh, as Finance Minister under Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao's government, presented the landmark July 1991 budget that launched India's economic reforms. His budget speech quoted Victor Hugo: 'No power on earth can stop an idea whose time has come.' As Finance Minister, he implemented: devaluation, fiscal consolidation, industrial delicensing, and trade liberalisation. Dr. Manmohan Singh, along with PM Narasimha Rao and Chief Economic Advisor Montek Singh Ahluwalia, is credited as the architect of India's economic reforms.

questions.question: The concept of 'Creative Destruction' (Joseph Schumpeter) is relevant to India's privatisation because:
options: Privatisation destroys PSUs creatively through sale
Privatisation and market competition destroy inefficient firms and sectors while enabling resources to shift to more productive uses — a process essential for dynamic economic growth
Government creatively finds new ways to fund PSUs through destruction of assets
Creative industries (arts, culture) were destroyed by privatisation
questions.explanation: Schumpeter's Creative Destruction: Capitalism advances through constant innovation — new products, processes, and firms emerge while old ones are destroyed. Applied to India's privatisation: When loss-making PSUs are privatised or closed, resources (capital, labour, land) are freed up for more productive uses. While painful in the short term (job losses, stranded communities), this reallocation is necessary for dynamic efficiency. The automobile industry example: closure of inefficient Ambassador car production freed resources for modern car manufacturing; the telecom example: BSNL's loss of monopoly allowed mobile revolution. LPG reforms triggered creative destruction — painful but necessary for growth acceleration.

questions.question: If India's Forex reserves were $5.8 billion in 1992 and grew to $600 billion by 2020 (28 years), calculate the CAGR. (Use: (600/5.8) ≈ 103.4; 103.4^(1/28) ≈ 1.175)
options: Approximately 17.5% per annum
Approximately 10% per annum
Approximately 25% per annum
Approximately 8% per annum
questions.explanation: CAGR = (End Value / Start Value)^(1/n) − 1 = (600/5.8)^(1/28) − 1 = (103.4)^(1/28) − 1. Using the given approximation: 103.4^(1/28) ≈ 1.175. CAGR = 1.175 − 1 = 0.175 = 17.5% per annum. India's forex reserves grew at approximately 17.5% CAGR over 28 years, one of the most dramatic transformations from a country in crisis to having the 4th largest forex reserves globally. This reflects sustained current account surpluses in services, strong remittances, and capital inflows from FDI and portfolio investment.

questions.question: The 'Linder Hypothesis' challenges H-O theory in explaining India's post-1991 trade pattern because it predicts:
options: Countries trade most with those that have similar factor endowments
Countries trade most intensively with other countries at a similar level of development and income, as demand structures are similar — explaining India's growing trade with OECD countries
Countries only trade in goods where they have absolute advantage
Trade between developing countries (South-South trade) dominates global trade flows
questions.explanation: Linder Hypothesis (Staffan Burenstam Linder, 1961): Trade in manufactured goods is most intensive between countries with similar per capita income levels because: (1) A product is developed to serve the domestic market first, (2) Countries with similar incomes have similar demand structures, (3) Therefore, most intense trade occurs between similar-income countries. For India: H-O would predict India trading most with capital-abundant USA (exporting labour-intensive goods). Linder adds: India's IT services export success to USA partly reflects the large US software services demand that created the market for India's IT exports — the US domestic IT market (Linder's 'representative demand') drove Indian IT exports. This hybrid explanation better fits India's actual trade pattern.

questions.question: The Indian government privatised VSNL (Videsh Sanchar Nigam Limited) in 2002, selling controlling stake to Tata Group. VSNL's monopoly on international telephony was ended. Call rates fell from ₹60/minute to ₹2/minute within 5 years. Identify the welfare analysis of this privatisation:
options: Pure welfare gain — consumers saved ₹58/minute with no offsetting losses

Complex welfare change: consumers gained enormously (consumer surplus increase), former monopoly profits shifted, government received disinvestment proceeds, and international communication explosion created broader economic benefits
Welfare loss — government lost monopoly profits that funded social programmes
Neutral welfare impact — monopoly profits and consumer surplus are equivalent
questions.explanation: VSNL privatisation welfare analysis: CONSUMER SURPLUS GAIN: Price fell from ₹60 to ₹2/minute — enormous consumer surplus increase; also, quantity of calls increased dramatically (demand response to lower prices). PRODUCER SURPLUS: VSNL's monopoly rent eliminated — but Tata Group's efficient management restored profitability. GOVERNMENT REVENUE: Disinvestment proceeds (₹1,439 crore) + subsequent dividends and taxes from profitable company. BROADER ECONOMIC BENEFITS: Cheap international calls facilitated IT sector growth, overseas remittances, NRI business connections, and educational access. This is a strong case study for privatisation + competition benefits — the VSNL experience is often cited as India's most successful privatisation.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's Human Development Index (HDI) rank improved post-1991 reforms. Reason (R): Economic growth from LPG reforms generated resources for increased public spending on health and education, directly improving HDI components.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is only partially correct — HDI improvement also required deliberate social spending decisions
A is false — India's HDI rank worsened post-1991
A is true but R is false — social spending fell post-1991 due to fiscal consolidation
questions.explanation: Assertion A is TRUE: India's HDI improved from 0.436 (1991) to 0.645 (2019), and rank improved (though still in Medium HDI category). Reason R is PARTIALLY true: Growth did generate more resources. However, the automatic 'trickle-up to social spending' mechanism was not guaranteed: (1) Fiscal consolidation in early 1990s actually REDUCED public spending on health and education as % of GDP initially, (2) HDI improvements required deliberate policy choices — Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, National Rural Health Mission, MGNREGA, mid-day meal scheme, (3) Economic growth alone without social policy would not have improved health/education outcomes equally. Therefore R is only partially correct — institutional choices were as important as growth.

questions.question: Dani Rodrik's critique of the 'Washington Consensus' is most relevant to India's 1991 reforms because he argues:
options: Developing countries should reject globalisation entirely and pursue autarky

Institutional quality and context-specific policies matter more than universal 'one-size-fits-all' reform prescriptions — and India should have designed its own reform path based on domestic conditions
Only trade liberalisation matters; other reforms are secondary
Capital account liberalisation should precede trade liberalisation
questions.explanation: Dani Rodrik's critique of Washington Consensus: 'Reform blueprints' prescribed by IMF/World Bank ignore the importance of context-specific institutions and local conditions. His key argument: What works in one country (or even in theory) may not work universally because: (1) Second-best theory — in the presence of multiple distortions, removing one distortion may not improve welfare, (2) Institutional complementarities — reforms work when supporting institutions exist, (3) Sequencing matters — opening capital account before strengthening banking regulation causes crises, (4) Political economy — reforms must be politically sustainable. For India: IMF's standardised conditionalities didn't account for India's specific structure (large informal economy, unique federal system, democratic constraints) — Rodrik argues India's selective, pragmatic approach was actually more sensible than full Washington Consensus compliance.

questions.question: India's external debt was $83 billion in 1991. If annual interest rate averaged 6% and India's export earnings were $18 billion, calculate the Debt Service Ratio (DSR = Annual Debt Service / Export Earnings × 100). If principal repayment was $5 billion and interest was $4.98 billion, what was the DSR, and is it within the safe zone (below 20%)?
options: DSR = 55%; Above safe zone — crisis level
DSR = 40%; Above safe zone — dangerously high
DSR = 27.7%; Slightly above safe zone of 20%
DSR = 15%; Within safe zone
questions.explanation: Debt Service = Annual principal repayment + Annual interest = $5 billion + $4.98 billion = $9.98 billion ≈ $10 billion. Export Earnings = $18 billion. DSR = ($10 billion / $18 billion) × 100 = 55.6% ≈ 55%. Safe zone criterion: DSR should ideally be below 20%. India's 55% DSR in 1991 was extremely high — almost triple the safe level — meaning India was paying more than half its export earnings just to service debt. This was a key indicator of the debt crisis that contributed to the 1991 BoP crisis. Post-reform, as exports grew dramatically, DSR fell to sustainable levels (below 15% by 2005).

questions.question: The 'Crowding In' effect — opposite of crowding out — can occur post-fiscal consolidation when:
options: Government increases public investment, creating demand for private sector goods
Reduction in fiscal deficit lowers interest rates, making private investment cheaper and encouraging private sector activity
Government crowds in foreign investment through SEZs
Lower fiscal deficit reduces inflation, increasing consumer spending
questions.explanation: Crowding In mechanism: When fiscal deficit is REDUCED, government's demand for credit decreases. With lower government borrowing demand, interest rates fall (price of credit falls). Lower interest rates make private investment cheaper — more investment projects become profitable. This stimulates private investment, 'crowding in' rather than crowding out. This is the supply-side mechanism underlying IMF's fiscal consolidation recommendations: short-term contraction (fiscal austerity) triggers medium-term expansion (lower interest rates → private investment → growth). India's post-1991 experience partially supported this — interest rates did fall from 12% to 6-7% as fiscal deficit reduced, stimulating private investment from 11% to 27% of GDP.

questions.question: The term 'Golden Handshake' in the context of India's privatisation refers to:
options: The agreement between India and the IMF for the 1991 loan
A generous voluntary retirement scheme (VRS) offered to employees of PSUs undergoing privatisation or downsizing
The handshake agreement between Manmohan Singh and IMF chief in 1991
Partnership agreements between Indian and foreign firms post-liberalisation
questions.explanation: Golden Handshake refers to the Voluntary Retirement Scheme (VRS) — a generous financial package offered to employees who voluntarily retire early. In the context of privatisation, this is used to reduce workforce of PSUs without forced retrenchment. Package typically includes: enhanced retirement benefits, one-time payment, medical benefits, etc. The 'voluntary' aspect mitigates the social and political cost of workforce reduction. Major VRS exercises: BSNL, Air India, Steel Authority of India, and nationalised banks offered VRS to reduce 'surplus labour' accumulated under public sector employment guarantees.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's services sector becoming 55%+ of GDP represents a 'premature deindustrialisation' — a development concern. Reason (R): Standard development trajectories show countries typically industrialise (manufacturing GDP share rises) before transitioning to services. India 'jumped' to services without adequate industrialisation, potentially limiting its labour absorption and productivity convergence.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but India's services-led growth was deliberate and superior to manufacturing-led growth
A is false — services-led growth is always superior to manufacturing-led growth
A is true but R is false — India had full industrialisation before services growth
questions.explanation: The 'premature deindustrialisation' thesis (Dani Rodrik): Standard development sequence: Agriculture → Manufacturing → Services. Manufacturing is important because: (1) Higher productivity than agriculture, enabling labour reallocation gains, (2) Tradeable sector with global technology access, (3) Labour-intensive at early stages (absorbs large numbers of unskilled workers). India appears to have jumped to services without adequate manufacturing — manufacturing GDP share never exceeded 16%, while comparable countries reached 25-30%. Reason R correctly identifies the development economics concern: without a manufacturing phase, India has fewer employment opportunities for the 300 million workers who need to move from agriculture, and services growth (being skill-intensive) cannot absorb this scale.

questions.question: India imposed an 'anti-dumping duty' of ₹8 per unit on Chinese steel imported at ₹40 per unit (claim: fair value is ₹48). If domestic steel costs ₹52 per unit and Indian demand is 100 million units, of which 40 million units are imported, how much does the anti-dumping duty change the competitive position?
options: After duty, Chinese steel costs ₹48 — still ₹4 cheaper than domestic; duty partially helps domestic producers
After duty, Chinese steel costs ₹48 = fair value; domestic at ₹52 still uncompetitive
After duty, Chinese steel costs ₹48 — making domestic steel equally competitive
After duty, Chinese steel costs ₹48 — domestic producers gain full 100 million unit market
questions.explanation: Pre-duty: Chinese steel = ₹40, Domestic steel = ₹52. Price difference = ₹12 (domestic is more expensive). Post anti-dumping duty of ₹8: Chinese steel = ₹40 + ₹8 = ₹48. Domestic steel = ₹52. New price difference = ₹52 − ₹48 = ₹4 (domestic still more expensive). The anti-dumping duty narrowed the price gap from ₹12 to ₹4 — partially improving competitive position for domestic producers but not making them fully competitive. Domestic producers still need to reduce costs by ₹4 to match the import price. This illustrates the graduated approach to anti-dumping protection — not removing all competition, but eliminating unfair pricing advantage.

questions.question: The 'Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act' (FRBM Act, 2003) was enacted as a follow-up to 1991 reforms primarily to:
options: Allow the government to increase fiscal deficit during recessions
Institutionalise fiscal discipline by legally mandating reduction of fiscal deficit and elimination of revenue deficit over a specified period
Require parliamentary approval for all foreign loans
Ensure transparency in PSU disinvestment proceeds
questions.explanation: FRBM Act 2003 was a landmark fiscal legislation: OBJECTIVES: (1) Eliminate revenue deficit by 2008-09, (2) Reduce fiscal deficit to 3% of GDP by 2008-09, (3) Ensure intergenerational equity by preventing debt accumulation, (4) Improve transparency through medium-term fiscal policy statements. SIGNIFICANCE: It institutionalised the fiscal consolidation initiated in 1991 — making it rule-based rather than politically discretionary. The Act embodied the lesson from 1991: fiscal profligacy creates BoP crises. FRBM targets were disrupted by Global Financial Crisis (2008-09 stimulus) and COVID (2020) but provided the framework. The N.K. Singh Committee (2017) revised FRBM to incorporate escape clauses for crises.

questions.question: The 'New Growth Theory' (Romer, Lucas) suggests that LPG reforms can lead to SUSTAINED (not just one-time) growth improvements because:
options: Physical capital accumulation from FDI leads to temporary growth acceleration followed by diminishing returns
Knowledge, technology, and human capital have increasing returns — globalisation facilitates technology spillovers and knowledge diffusion, potentially permanently raising India's growth rate
Trade liberalisation raises the marginal product of capital permanently
Lower tariffs increase consumer spending, permanently boosting aggregate demand
questions.explanation: New Growth Theory (Romer 1990, Lucas 1988) differs from neoclassical growth theory: NEOCLASSICAL: Capital accumulation leads to growth but with diminishing returns, eventually reaching steady state — only technological progress (exogenous) sustains long-run growth. NEW GROWTH THEORY: Technology and knowledge have increasing returns and positive externalities (knowledge spillovers). Globalisation can permanently raise growth by: (1) Access to global technology frontier through FDI, (2) Learning-by-exporting — export competition forces innovation, (3) Scale economies — global market access increases returns to R&D, (4) Human capital development through education systems responding to global demands. For India: IT sector exemplifies how knowledge spillovers (Bangalore ecosystem) create self-reinforcing growth clusters consistent with new growth theory.

questions.question: A trade economist analyses India's merchandise export basket: in 1991, petroleum products constituted 3%, gems and jewellery 18%, textiles 23%, engineering goods 14%, other 42%. By 2018: petroleum products 14%, engineering goods 26%, chemicals 13%, gems 15%, textiles 12%, other 20%. What trend does this reveal about India's export sophistication post-liberalisation?
options: Export basket became more concentrated — less diversification
Export basket diversified and moved up the value chain — engineering and chemicals (higher value-added) gained share while textiles (lower value-added) declined relatively
Export basket deteriorated — all traditional items lost share
No change — India's export basket remained static post-liberalisation
questions.explanation: The 1991-2018 export basket transformation shows: (1) Engineering goods: 14% → 26% — significant gain, reflecting manufacturing capability development, (2) Chemicals/pharmaceuticals: grew substantially — India became a global generic pharma leader, (3) Textiles: 23% → 12% — relative decline (still large in absolute terms), (4) Petroleum products: 3% → 14% — India's refinery capacity growth (Jamnagar refinery). This represents EXPORT DIVERSIFICATION and UPGRADING: export basket moved from traditional labour-intensive goods (textiles) toward higher value-added engineering goods and knowledge-intensive pharmaceuticals. This is consistent with dynamic comparative advantage development — India's exports became more complex over time, consistent with Hausmann's 'product space' framework.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's current account deficit (CAD) widened after 2000s despite reforms, reaching 4.8% of GDP in 2012-13. Reason (R): India's merchandise imports (especially oil, gold, and capital goods) grew faster than exports, while software and remittance surpluses were insufficient to compensate.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false — CAD remained below 1% throughout post-reform period
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is TRUE: India's CAD widened from manageable levels (1-2% GDP) to 4.8% of GDP in 2012-13 — a level that triggered currency crisis and rupee depreciation. Reason R correctly explains this: (1) Oil imports grew with rising crude prices ($25/barrel in 2001 to $110/barrel in 2012), (2) Gold imports surged (India imports 800-1000 tonnes annually for jewellery/investment), (3) Capital goods imports for infrastructure and manufacturing, (4) Merchandise trade deficit reached $190 billion. While IT exports (~$100 billion) and remittances (~$70 billion) were strong, they couldn't fully offset the merchandise deficit. This shows that even with successful services exports, managing the CAD requires addressing structural import dependence.

questions.question: A country's exchange rate is ₹70/$ in year 1 (base). Domestic inflation = 8%, US inflation = 3%. Using Purchasing Power Parity (PPP), calculate the expected exchange rate in year 2. Additionally, if the actual exchange rate is ₹73/$, is the rupee overvalued or undervalued?
options: PPP rate = ₹73.3/$; Actual rate ₹73/$ → rupee slightly overvalued
PPP rate = ₹75.5/$; Actual ₹73/$ → rupee undervalued
PPP rate = ₹73.3/$; Actual ₹73/$ → rupee very slightly undervalued
PPP rate = ₹70/$; No change as PPP holds perfectly
questions.explanation: PPP Exchange Rate formula: E(Year 2) = E(Year 1) × (1 + domestic inflation) / (1 + foreign inflation) = ₹70 × (1.08 / 1.03) = ₹70 × 1.0485 = ₹73.39 ≈ ₹73.4/$. Actual rate = ₹73/$. Comparison: PPP suggests ₹73.4/$, but actual is ₹73/$. Actual rate has fewer rupees per dollar than PPP predicts, meaning the rupee is STRONGER than PPP → rupee is OVERVALUED by approximately ₹0.4 (small overvaluation). An overvalued rupee means Indian exports are relatively more expensive for foreigners and imports cheaper — hurting export competitiveness. This PPP-based assessment is a key tool for evaluating exchange rate policy impact on trade.

questions.question: The 'GATS Mode 4' (General Agreement on Trade in Services Mode 4) is significant for India because it relates to:
options: Cross-border supply of services through internet (India's IT exports)
Movement of natural persons — temporary migration of service professionals across borders — directly relevant to India's IT and healthcare worker exports to US/UK/Australia
Commercial presence — foreign service firms establishing subsidiaries in India
Consumption abroad — Indian patients going to Thailand for medical treatment
questions.explanation: GATS Mode 4: Temporary Movement of Natural Persons — service providers crossing borders to deliver services. India's Mode 4 interests: (1) IT professionals on H-1B visas working in US companies, (2) Nurses and doctors working in UK's NHS, (3) Hospitality workers, (4) Accountants and engineers on temporary assignments. India is the world's largest Mode 4 exporter — IT workforce mobility is central to its services export model. India has been a strong advocate for more liberal Mode 4 commitments in WTO negotiations, as developed countries have been reluctant to liberalise immigration policies even while demanding financial services liberalisation under other modes.

questions.question: Which of the following MOST accurately describes the 'J-curve effect' in the context of India's 1991 currency devaluation?
options: GDP initially falls then rises after devaluation
Trade balance initially worsens after devaluation (as import bill rises faster than export revenue) before improving as trade volumes adjust over 6-18 months
Foreign exchange reserves initially rise then fall after devaluation
Fiscal deficit rises then falls in J-curve pattern after devaluation
questions.explanation: J-curve effect in trade: When a currency is devalued: IMMEDIATE IMPACT (0-6 months): Trade balance WORSENS. Why? Import prices rise immediately (in domestic currency), but import volumes don't fall immediately — long-term contracts, essential goods, adjustment lags. Export prices fall in foreign currency, but export volumes don't rise immediately — new orders need time. So import value increases, export value stagnates → trade deficit widens. MEDIUM-TERM (6-18 months): Trade volumes adjust — imports fall, exports rise → trade balance IMPROVES. This J-shaped path (down then up) explains why India's trade deficit initially worsened after the 1991 devaluation before improving. The Marshall-Lerner condition (sum of import and export elasticities > 1) must hold for eventual improvement.

questions.question: In 2008, when the US financial crisis caused global recession, India's growth fell from 9% to 6.7% in 2008-09. By 2010-11, India's growth recovered to 8.4%. China also fell from 13% to 9% growth. Contrast with countries like Iceland and Ireland that faced near-collapse. What does India's resilience in 2008 demonstrate about the nature of globalisation's risks?
options: India proved that globalisation has no risks for large economies

India's transmission of the crisis through trade and FII channels showed vulnerability, but India's limited financial integration (managed capital account, limited toxic asset exposure) provided insulation compared to fully integrated economies
India was not affected by globalisation during 2008 crisis
India's agricultural sector protected it from financial globalisation risks
questions.explanation: India's 2008 crisis experience illustrates differential globalisation risk: TRANSMISSION CHANNELS: (1) Trade channel — export demand fell as US/EU receded, (2) FII channel — $13 billion FII withdrawal in 2008, rupee fell 20%, (3) Remittances — Gulf remittances briefly fell, (4) Credit tightening — global credit crunch. INSULATION FACTORS: (1) Limited toxic asset (CDO/MBS) exposure — Indian banks couldn't hold these, (2) Capital controls on FII reduced contagion speed, (3) Large domestic consumption base (economy less export-dependent than China/East Asia), (4) RBI's macroprudential policies. Comparison: Iceland's banking collapse, Ireland's property/banking crisis showed how FULL financial integration (capital account, banking integration) amplified the crisis. India's 'managed globalisation' provided meaningful insulation.

questions.question: India's total IT-BPO exports were $150 billion in 2018-19. If this represents 6% of global IT spending of $2,500 billion, and India aims to capture 10% market share by increasing exports by ₹x billion, what additional export value is needed? How many jobs at $40,000 average salary would this create if labour-to-revenue ratio is 60%?
options: Additional exports needed = $100 billion; Jobs created = 15,00,000
Additional exports needed = $250 billion; Jobs created = 37,50,000
Additional exports needed = $100 billion; Jobs created = 37,50,000
Additional exports needed = $250 billion; Jobs created = 15,00,000
questions.explanation: Target market share = 10% of $2,500 billion = $250 billion. Current IT exports = $150 billion. Additional exports needed = $250 − $150 = $100 billion. Labour costs as 60% of revenue from additional exports = 60% × $100 billion = $60 billion in labour costs. Jobs created = Total labour cost / Average salary = $60 billion / $40,000 = $60,000,000,000 / $40,000 = 1,500,000 = 15 lakh (1.5 million) additional jobs. This analysis shows the employment potential of expanding India's IT sector market share — 15 lakh high-quality jobs at above-average salaries, directly linked to globalisation and trade policy in services.

questions.question: Under India's 1991 industrial policy, 'automatic route' for FDI meant:
options: FDI proposals were automatically rejected unless specifically approved
Foreign investors could invest up to specified equity limits without prior government approval, only requiring notification to RBI
FDI was automatically taxed at a standard rate without negotiation
Foreign companies automatically received Navratna status upon investing
questions.explanation: Automatic Route for FDI (established 1991, expanded over time): Foreign investors can invest up to the sectoral FDI cap (which varies by sector) WITHOUT requiring prior approval from the Foreign Investment Promotion Board (FIPB) or government. The investor only needs to notify RBI within 30 days of receiving investment. Example: FDI up to 74% in telecom (earlier 49%, progressively raised) was on automatic route. Approval Route: sectors requiring government/FIPB approval — sensitive sectors like defence, media, multi-brand retail. This distinction was central to liberalising FDI — removing bureaucratic approval requirements for most sectors streamlined investment processes significantly.

questions.question: The 'twin deficits hypothesis' as applied to India's pre-1991 crisis argues:
options: India had both fiscal and trade deficits, which were structurally related — high fiscal deficit drove domestic absorption above production, creating current account deficit
India had both agricultural and industrial production deficits
India had deficits in both democratic governance and economic governance simultaneously
India had deficits in both infrastructure and education simultaneously
questions.explanation: Twin Deficits Hypothesis (Friedman/Mundell-Fleming): A country's fiscal deficit and current account deficit are causally linked: HIGH FISCAL DEFICIT → Government borrows and spends → Total domestic absorption (consumption + investment + government spending) > total domestic production → The gap must be filled by IMPORTS → CURRENT ACCOUNT DEFICIT. The math: CAD = Private investment − Private savings − (Government revenue − Government expenditure). If government runs large deficit (G > T), and private savings = investment, CAD must be large. India 1991: Fiscal deficit 8.4% of GDP led to similar-magnitude CAD, validating the twin deficits hypothesis. IMF's prescription: reduce fiscal deficit to reduce CAD.

questions.question: Assertion (A): India's WTO membership restricted its ability to provide subsidies to domestic industries, making industrial policy more difficult post-1991. Reason (R): WTO's Agreement on Subsidies and Countervailing Measures (ASCM) prohibits export subsidies and restricts domestic subsidies ('actionable subsidies') that distort trade.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but India has exemptions as a developing country under WTO
A is true but R is false — WTO rules on subsidies are non-binding
A is false — WTO membership gave India new freedom to subsidise exports
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true, but with an important qualification: Assertion A is TRUE: WTO membership did constrain India's subsidy policy space. Reason R is TRUE: ASCM prohibits export subsidies and certain domestic subsidies. HOWEVER — as a developing country (Annex VII of ASCM), India historically had special and differential treatment (SDT): exemption from export subsidy prohibition if per capita GNP below $1,000, flexibility in domestic subsidies, longer phase-out periods. India graduated out of some SDT provisions as it became more developed. India has faced countervailing duty cases from US and EU on various subsidies. The full statement B is correct: both A and R are true, but R must be understood with the SDT qualification for developing countries.

questions.question: If India's GDP growth is 7% and population growth is 1.2%, calculate per capita GDP growth. If initial per capita GDP is $1,000, what is per capita GDP after 10 years at this growth rate? (Use: (1.057)^10 ≈ 1.745)
options: Per capita GDP growth ≈ 5.73%; Per capita GDP after 10 years ≈ $1,745
Per capita GDP growth ≈ 7%; Per capita GDP after 10 years ≈ $2,000
Per capita GDP growth ≈ 5.73%; Per capita GDP after 10 years ≈ $1,500
Per capita GDP growth ≈ 8.2%; Per capita GDP after 10 years ≈ $2,200
questions.explanation: Per capita GDP growth rate = (1 + GDP growth) / (1 + population growth) − 1 = (1.07 / 1.012) − 1 = 1.0573 − 1 = 0.0573 = 5.73%. Per capita GDP after 10 years = $1,000 × (1.0573)^10 ≈ $1,000 × 1.745 = $1,745. This shows why population management matters alongside economic growth — India's demographic challenge means 7% GDP growth translates to only ~5.7% per capita improvement. Countries with lower population growth (China ~0.5%) get much more benefit from the same GDP growth rate in per capita terms.

questions.question: The 'Resource Curse' hypothesis might paradoxically apply to India's IT success if:
options: India's IT sector depletes natural resources

IT sector success generates forex inflows and appreciation, potentially reducing competitiveness of labour-intensive manufacturing — similar to how oil windfalls hurt other sectors in resource-rich countries
IT companies exploit India's natural human resources
Resources spent on IT education crowd out technical education for manufacturing
questions.explanation: The IT-as-resource-curse hypothesis: Classical Resource Curse — countries with oil/mineral booms see Dutch Disease effects (currency appreciation, crowding out of manufacturing). Analogously, India's software export boom: (1) Generated massive dollar inflows (IT exports $150+ billion annually), (2) Created upward pressure on rupee (RBI had to buy dollars to prevent sharp appreciation), (3) Real wage inflation in IT sector raised economy-wide wage expectations, (4) Educated talent concentrated in IT over manufacturing engineering. These effects 'crowded out' labour-intensive manufacturing — the sector that typically absorbs large numbers of low-skill workers. India's manufacturing sector remained weak despite overall growth — a pattern consistent with a soft version of resource curse dynamics driven by services rather than commodities.

questions.question: In 2016, India undertook demonetisation of ₹500 and ₹1,000 notes. FDI regulations were simultaneously simplified. A foreign investor observes: (1) short-term economic disruption, (2) long-term formalisation of economy. How does this episode relate to the LPG reform framework?
options: Demonetisation is a component of privatisation policy

While not strictly a LPG reform, demonetisation complements liberalisation by reducing the shadow economy, improving tax compliance, and expanding formal financial inclusion — enabling better market functioning
Demonetisation contradicts LPG reforms by increasing government intervention
Demonetisation is a globalisation measure to attract FDI
questions.explanation: Demonetisation and LPG framework: Demonetisation (Nov 8, 2016) was not a classical liberalisation, privatisation, or globalisation measure. However, it connects to the broader reform agenda: (1) Formalisation — reducing cash economy brings more transactions into the formal sector, enabling better regulatory oversight and market efficiency (liberalisation's goal), (2) Tax compliance — more digital transactions improve tax collection, reducing fiscal deficit, (3) Digital payments infrastructure — UPI growth post-demonetisation accelerated financial inclusion, (4) Concurrent FDI simplification (100% FDI in food retail) is direct liberalisation. The connection is indirect but real — demonetisation aimed to improve institutional quality of markets, which complements but is distinct from LPG reforms.

questions.question: The 'Feldstein-Horioka Puzzle' observes a high correlation between domestic savings and domestic investment across countries. India's growing current account deficit post-liberalisation (implying savings-investment gap financed by capital imports) indicates:
options: India disproved the Feldstein-Horioka puzzle — capital is NOT internationally mobile in India

India's increasing capital account openness post-liberalisation allowed increasing divergence between domestic savings and investment — partially globalising India's capital market and enabling higher investment than domestic savings permit
India confirmed the Feldstein-Horioka puzzle by keeping savings equal to investment
The puzzle is irrelevant to India's experience
questions.explanation: Feldstein-Horioka Puzzle (1980): Found high correlation between domestic savings rate and domestic investment rate across OECD countries — suggesting capital is NOT freely mobile internationally (contradicting open economy theory). India's experience: Pre-liberalisation: Capital controls kept savings ≈ investment (Feldstein-Horioka holds). Post-liberalisation: FDI and FII inflows allowed India's investment rate (38% of GDP) to exceed domestic savings rate (32% of GDP) — the 6% gap financed by capital imports (current account deficit).
